# Testing Data Analysis Workflows {#sec-testing}

::: {.callout-note}
## Sources

PHB 243B Lecture 8 (`slides.qmd`, `scripts/`); blog post
`40-testingfordataanalysisworkflow`; the `testthat` package.
:::

## Prerequisites

Answer the following questions to see if you can bypass this
chapter. You can find the answers at the end of the chapter in
@sec-quiz-answers-20.

1. Why would you test a one-off data analysis script that
   will only ever run on a single dataset?
2. What is the difference between a unit test, an
   integration test, and an end-to-end test in the context
   of a data analysis pipeline?
3. What does `testthat::expect_snapshot()` capture, and
   when is a snapshot test preferable to a direct
   value-comparison test?

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you should be able to:

- Write unit tests for analytic helper functions with
  `testthat`.
- Add an integration test that exercises the full data
  pipeline on a small synthetic dataset.
- Use `expect_snapshot()` to capture complex outputs
  (printed objects, plots, summary tables).
- Run the test suite locally via `testthat::test_local()`
  and in CI via GitHub Actions.
- Identify test smells (tests that pass for the wrong
  reason, tests that hide bugs, flaky tests).

## Orientation

Tests are how you convince a reader (including future-you)
that your code actually does what you think it does.
Analysis code rarely gets tests, and the lack of them is a
major source of reproducibility failures. This chapter
brings the habits of software engineering to bear on the
practice of data analysis.

## Why test analyses?

TODO: three reasons: catch regressions after refactors,
document expected behaviour, catch upstream data changes.

## Unit tests with testthat

TODO: `test_that()` blocks, `expect_equal`,
`expect_error`, `expect_true`.

## Integration tests on synthetic data

TODO: creating a small fake dataset that exercises every
branch; running the pipeline end-to-end in under a second.

## Snapshot tests

TODO: `expect_snapshot()`, `expect_snapshot_value()`, when
to accept a change and when to investigate.

## Testing plots

TODO: `vdiffr::expect_doppelganger()` for visual
regression testing.

## Continuous integration

TODO: `usethis::use_github_action_check_standard()`; a
minimal workflow that runs tests on every push.

## Test smells

TODO: tautological tests, brittle snapshots, overly
permissive tolerances, tests that mock the thing being
tested.

## With AI assistance {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prompts to try

1. Paste an analytic helper function and ask the LLM to
   write `testthat` tests that 'cover the edge cases'.
   Delete a line from the function and re-run the tests;
   do they catch the regression?
2. Paste a data-cleaning pipeline and ask the LLM to write
   an integration test on a small synthetic dataset.
   Check: does the test actually exercise every cleaning
   step, or only the happy path?
3. Ask the LLM to diagnose a flaky test (pastes the test
   plus its random failures). Does it identify the source
   of non-determinism?
:::

## Exercises

1. Add unit tests for two functions from an existing
   analysis of yours. Aim for at least: a happy-path
   test, an edge-case test, and an error-path test.
2. Write an integration test: a 20-line synthetic dataset
   that exercises every branch of your pipeline. Run the
   full pipeline in under a second.
3. Set up GitHub Actions with
   `usethis::use_github_action_check_standard()`. Push
   and verify the workflow runs green.

## Further reading

- [@wickham2023rpkgs] testing chapters --- `testthat` canonical reference.
- `vdiffr` documentation on CRAN --- visual regression testing for `ggplot2`.

## Prerequisites answers {#sec-quiz-answers-20}

1. Even a one-off analysis benefits from tests: they
   catch regressions when you refactor mid-analysis,
   they serve as executable documentation of expected
   behaviour, and they detect silent failures when
   upstream data changes format. The cost of writing a
   handful of tests is small; the cost of silently
   mis-analysing data is much larger.
2. A unit test exercises a single function with known
   inputs and checks the output. An integration test
   runs a pipeline segment (multiple functions) on
   synthetic data and checks the pipeline's output. An
   end-to-end test runs the full analysis pipeline from
   raw data to final figures/tables. Unit tests are fast
   and narrow; end-to-end tests are slow and broad;
   integration tests sit in between.
3. `expect_snapshot()` captures the output of an
   expression (printed text, numerical result, plot) to
   a file on first run. On subsequent runs it compares
   the new output against the saved snapshot. Use it
   when the output is complex (a printed object, a plot,
   a formatted table) and not easily expressed as a
   literal value. Review changes manually when they
   occur.
